articles.title: Four Jersey cows die from pneumonia after heatwave damage - BBC
articles.search_term: (heatwave OR "heat wave" OR "severe heatwave" OR "extreme heat" OR "heat spell" OR "scorching heat" OR "blistering heat" OR "sweltering heat" OR "hot winds" OR loo OR "hot loo winds" OR "intense heat" OR "unbearable heat" OR "deadly heat" OR heat OR "weather forecast" OR "weather update" OR "weather alert" OR "IMD forecast" OR "IMD prediction" OR "IMD alert" OR IMD OR "rising temperatures" OR "temperatures expected to rise" OR "relief from heat" OR "respite from heat" OR "heat respite" OR "respite from scorching heat") Andaman and Nicobar Islands
articles.full_text:
- Published
Four cows have died after their immune systems were damaged by record-breaking heat, a farm has confirmed.
Clairval Organic Dairy Farm said several of its calves became unwell with pneumonia after June's heatwave, which saw the island's warmest temperatures since 1894.
"Despite the dedicated care of our veterinary team and everyone here at Clairval, in the past week we've sadly lost Leonidas, Ahsoka, Maybelle and Dream Lady," the farm said.
It added: "They were loved, they were my joy every morning when I entered the barn, they will be deeply missed. Sleep peacefully, little ones."
The farm said heat stress could weaken a calf's immune system, leaving it vulnerable to infection in the days that follow.
Last month, Jersey Met said temperatures soared to a record-breaking 39.3°C (102.7F).
The heatwave between 21 to 25 June had four consecutive days with temperatures of 30C (86F) or more.

articles.title: Summer workout hacks: How to stay safe under the Sun - The Times of India
articles.search_term: (heatstroke OR "heat stroke" OR sunstroke OR "heat exhaustion" OR "heat-related death" OR "heat casualty" OR "heat fatality" OR "suspected heat death" OR dehydration OR "heat cramps" OR "heat illness") Andaman and Nicobar Islands
articles.full_text:
The summer season is here and it is that time when many people avoid exercising, as this routine can be bad for your health! This is because when you exercise in hot weather, it is tough on your heart. Your heart has to work a lot to keep you cool when it is hot outside. Exercising in the summer heat is not easy on your system, as the summer heat makes your heart work harder. Without the right approach, a routine jog can easily spiral into heat exhaustion or heat stroke. Well, it just requires changing the way you think about exercise from pushing through the pain to working out. By knowing how your body deals with heat, you can change your routine to stay fit without putting yourself in danger.
How does heat affect things?
When you work out, your muscles make heat. To get rid of that heat, your body sends a lot of blood to your skin, where it makes sweat. This works really normally but high temperatures and humidity make it not work as well. If the air is hot and humid, the sweat cannot evaporate properly, which makes your body temperature go up.
What the research says:
A foundational study published in the Scandinavian Journal of Medicine & Science in Sports outlines how extreme heat fundamentally alters human athletic performance (Racinais et al.,
2015). The international consensus paper notes that when an individual exercises in hot environments, the body is forced to drastically increase skin blood flow and sweat rates to dump heat. However, this creates a major physiological conflict: the cardiovascular system struggles to simultaneously pump enough oxygen-rich blood to working muscles while diverting blood to the skin for cooling. The researchers concluded that this dual demand accelerates dehydration, spikes core body temperatures, and significantly degrades aerobic endurance capacity unless individuals undergo a structured 7-to-14-day heat acclimatization protocol.
Moreover, the researchers found out that getting too hot and not having water makes it much harder to do aerobic exercises and it feels like you are working way harder than you really are. In other words, your body has to work twice as hard to do half as much, which makes you get tired really fast and increases the risk of getting sick from the heat.
Rules for safe workouts
Master the Clock
Avoid exercising between 10:00 AM and 4:00 PM, when the sun's rays are most intense. Instead, lace up early in the morning or head out after dusk. Morning workouts are ideal because the ground hasn't had all day to absorb and radiate heat back at you.
Dress for the Occasion
Ditch the heavy cotton t-shirts, which trap moisture and heat against your skin. Instead, opt for lightweight, light-colored, and moisture-free fabrics. Light colors reflect the sun's rays rather than absorbing them, and technical fabrics help your sweat evaporate faster to keep you cool.
Hydrate before you sweat
Don't wait until you're thirsty to start drinking water; by then, you're already mildly dehydrated. Sip water throughout the day leading up to your workout. If you plan to exercise for more than 60 minutes, skip the plain water and reach for an electrolyte drink to replace the sodium and potassium lost in your sweat.
Time to acclimatize
Your body is remarkably adaptable, but it needs time to adjust to a sudden heatwave. It takes about 7 to 14 days of gradual exposure for your body to optimize its cooling systems (like increasing sweat rate and blood volume). Keep your workouts short and low-intensity for the first couple of weeks when the seasons shift.
Know the warning signs
Listen to your body. It will tell you when to stop. Small problems like heat cramps can get fast if you ignore them. Learn the warning signs of heat problems:
* Heat Exhaustion: You sweat a lot, your heart beats fast, and you feel dizzy, nauseous and weak. If you feel this way. Go to the shade. Drink water.
* Heat Stroke: Your body temperature goes above 103°F; you feel confused, faint or hot and dry. You might also vomit. This is an emergency. Get help away.
The Bottom Line
Working out in the heat is not about being tough. It's about being smart. On hot or humid days it's okay to move your workout to a gym with air conditioning. You can. Take a rest day. Be flexible drink water and listen to your body.

articles.title: Heat Exhaustion Strands Hikers on Hartman Bar Trail, Multi-Agency Rescue Lasts Nearly Nine Hours - SierraDailyNews.com
articles.search_term: (heatstroke OR "heat stroke" OR sunstroke OR "heat exhaustion" OR "heat-related death" OR "heat casualty" OR "heat fatality" OR "suspected heat death" OR dehydration OR "heat cramps" OR "heat illness") Andaman and Nicobar Islands
articles.full_text:
A nearly nine-hour backcountry rescue operation on July 3 highlighted the ongoing hazards of summer hiking conditions in Plumas County.
Plumas County Search and Rescue (PCSAR) was paged at about 7:15 p.m. for a group of five hikers on a remote trail near the Hartman Bar area who were reportedly out of water and showing signs of possible heat-related illness.
Crews from the U.S. Forest Service, already working in the area, were first to reach the group via a steep, rugged and heavily overgrown trail. Three of the hikers were able to hike out on their own before rescuers arrived. However, two members of the group were unable to continue due to exhaustion and symptoms consistent with heat stress.
Fire personnel and paramedics from the Plumas National Forest provided initial medical aid on scene as PCSAR volunteers arrived to assist. Crews then worked together to escort the remaining hikers safely down the trail and back to safety.
The operation stretched late into the evening, underscoring the difficulty of accessing remote terrain and the added risks posed by high summer temperatures.
Search and rescue officials used the incident as a reminder for backcountry travelers to carry more water than expected, avoid strenuous hiking during peak heat hours, and always pack the “Ten Essentials.” Officials also noted that many local trails have changed significantly since the Dixie and other major fires, with increased deadfall, erosion, and overgrown sections creating additional challenges.
Plumas County Search and Rescue is an all-volunteer organization and operates as a registered 501(c)(3) nonprofit.

articles.title: Fresh spell of rain exposes potholes across city; IMD issues red alert for Tuesday - The Times of India
articles.search_term: ("heat advisory" OR "heat action plan" OR "heat alert" OR "IMD warning" OR "IMD alert" OR "red alert" OR "orange alert" OR "yellow alert" OR "NDMA advisory" OR "cooling shelter" OR "relief measures" OR "heat compensation" OR "severe weather warning") Andaman and Nicobar Islands
articles.full_text:
Nashik: Fresh spell of rainfall over the past four days has exposed potholes on several roads across Nashik city, adding to the woes of the commuters and aggravating traffic congestion on stretches where road widening and upgrading works are underway ahead of the 2027 Kumbh Mela.
The deteriorating road conditions came into focus on Monday morning when a truck got stuck after the service road near Dwarka Circle caved in following heavy showers, disrupting traffic in the area.
Residents have complained that continuous rainfall has washed away road surfaces at several locations, particularly on stretches carrying diverted traffic due to ongoing infrastructure works.
The Nashik Municipal Corporation (NMC) has already launched road improvement works on 28 major roads covering around 150-km across the city as part of Kumbh Mela infrastructure projects.
As asphalt work cannot be carried out during the monsoon, the civic body has started filling potholes temporarily with Granular Sub-Base (GSB) material. However, the residents have criticized the method, alleging that GSB-filled potholes are washed away during heavy rain and fail to provide long-term relief.
An official from the NMC’s public works department said the civic body is left with limited options during the monsoon.
“We have started filling potholes with GSB material as a temporary measure since asphalt repair work cannot be undertaken during heavy rainfall. Once the monsoon recedes, permanent repairs and resurfacing works will be carried out,” the official said.
Residents, meanwhile, have urged the civic administration to adopt modern pothole-repair technologies such as cold mix solutions that can be used during the monsoon season. City resident Manoj Chaughule said the focus should be on durable solutions rather than temporary repairs.
“Using GSB material repeatedly is a waste of public money as the potholes reappear after every spell of rain. The civic body should use newer technologies that offer longer-lasting results,” he said.
Another resident, Pradeep Patil, pointed out that mud accumulation on several roads has increased the risk of accidents, especially for two-wheeler riders.
“Some roads have become slippery and muddy. The civic body should ensure regular cleaning and take immediate measures to make roads safer for the motorists,” Patil said.
Meanwhile, the India Meteorological Department (IMD) has issued a red alert for Nashik district on Tuesday, forecasting heavy to very heavy rainfall at several places and extremely heavy rainfall at isolated locations. A yellow alert has been issued for Wednesday.
The city continued to receive heavy rainfall for the fourth consecutive day on Monday. Nashik recorded 64.4 mm rainfall between Friday morning and Monday evening. The recent showers have also improved water availability, with 385 mcft inflow recorded in the catchment areas of Gangapur, Mukane and Darna dams, boosting water reserves for the city.

articles.title: IMD issues orange alert for very heavy rain in parts of MP - The Times of India
articles.search_term: ("heat advisory" OR "heat action plan" OR "heat alert" OR "IMD warning" OR "IMD alert" OR "red alert" OR "orange alert" OR "yellow alert" OR "NDMA advisory" OR "cooling shelter" OR "relief measures" OR "heat compensation" OR "severe weather warning") Andaman and Nicobar Islands
articles.full_text:
Bhopal: The
India Meteorological Department has issued an orange alert for very heavy rainfall in parts of Madhya Pradesh on Tuesday, as the monsoon system is expected to remain highly active over northern and central regions for the next three days.
Districts including Guna, Ashoknagar, Vidisha, Sagar and Tikamgarh are likely to receive very heavy rainfall on July 7. The focus of intense rain will shift to Bhind, Datia, Shivpuri, Guna and Ashoknagar on Wednesday. Bhind and Datia are expected to continue witnessing very heavy rain on Thursday.
Bhopal remains under a yellow warning for heavy rainfall with thunderstorms and lightning on Tuesday and Wednesday. The city is likely to see spells of moderate to heavy rain along with gusty winds at isolated places. The warning is expected to be downgraded on Thursday to thunderstorms and light to moderate rain as the system weakens.
Bhopal received evening showers on Monday, bringing relief from humidity and a dip in temperatures after sunset. Bhopal recorded a maximum temperature of 33.4°C during the day, 1.1°C higher than Sunday.
According to IMD, widespread rainfall will continue across MP through Wednesday, with heavy rain likely in central, eastern and south-eastern districts. Thunderstorms with lightning and winds of 40-50 kmph are also expected. From Thursday, rainfall is likely to reduce over western MP, while northern districts bordering Uttar Pradesh will remain under the influence of the active monsoon.

articles.title: Monsoon: Orange alert for next 2 days in Dharwad - The Times of India
articles.search_term: ("heat advisory" OR "heat action plan" OR "heat alert" OR "IMD warning" OR "IMD alert" OR "red alert" OR "orange alert" OR "yellow alert" OR "NDMA advisory" OR "cooling shelter" OR "relief measures" OR "heat compensation" OR "severe weather warning") Andaman and Nicobar Islands
articles.full_text:
Dharwad: Moderate to heavy rain lashed Dharwad district, including the twin cities of Hubballi and Dharwad, throughout Monday as the southwest monsoon made a strong comeback, bringing much-needed relief to farmers after a prolonged dry spell.
The rain, which began late on Sunday night, continued across all taluks of the district. While Hubballi witnessed a few intense spells, Dharwad city and the remaining taluks received steady rainfall from early morning until evening.
The persistent showers disrupted normal life, causing inconvenience to commuters, students and office-goers. Despite the disruption, rain has raised hopes among farmers, whose standing crops had come under stress due to inadequate rain in June.
According to the district disaster management authority, all taluks recorded above-normal rain on July 5 and 6. During the past seven days, the district received a cumulative rain of 35.8mm against the normal average of 30.4mm, indicating a significant improvement.
The fresh spell has also rejuvenated the district’s two major streams, Bennihalla and Tupparihalla, following substantial rainfall in their catchment areas, resulting in a steady rise in water flow.
Meanwhile, the India Meteorological Department (IMD) has issued an orange alert for Dharwad district, forecasting moderate to heavy rain over the next two days. District authorities are closely monitoring the situation as water levels in the streams continue to rise, and have urged the public to remain cautious, particularly in low-lying areas near the water bodies.

articles.title: Mumbai sees wettest start to monsoon since 2000 as IMD issues red alert - India Today
articles.search_term: ("heat advisory" OR "heat action plan" OR "heat alert" OR "IMD warning" OR "IMD alert" OR "red alert" OR "orange alert" OR "yellow alert" OR "NDMA advisory" OR "cooling shelter" OR "relief measures" OR "heat compensation" OR "severe weather warning") Andaman and Nicobar Islands
articles.full_text:
Mumbai sees wettest start to monsoon since 2000 as IMD issues red alert
By July 6, Mumbai had seen more rain than in any year since 2000, and the IMD's red alert warns of more. India Today's Data Intelligence Unit maps the rain and the risk beneath it.
Mumbai is bracing for more. The India Meteorological Department upgraded its warning for Mumbai, Thane, and Raigad to a red alert on Monday, forecasting heavy to very heavy rain and winds gusting to 80 kmph. The red alert runs until July 8; neighbouring Palghar is under an orange alert.
The warning caps a record-breaking start to the monsoon. By July 6, the city's grid had already logged about 1,240 mm of rain this season, the most for this date in 27 years, according to IMD gridded rainfall data analysed by India Today's Data Intelligence Unit. That is about 1.7 times what a normal early July delivers, and it edges past the previous high, set in 2006.
The rain is already biting. Seventeen flights were cancelled, and 365 were delayed at Mumbai airport on Monday, according to airport officials.
A RECORD YOU CAN WATCH BUILD, DAY BY DAY
Rank 2026 against the past 26 monsoons over the same June 1–July 6 window, and it sits first on almost every measure. Its total, about 1,240 mm, is the highest on record for the date. Its wettest five-day spell, more than 750 mm between July 2 and 6, is more than double the typical 350.
The rain is also falling harder per wet day, about 73 mm against a usual 35. By early July, the city had already logged two days that crossed the IMD's "extremely heavy" mark of 204.5 mm — a category that a typical year does not record this early. On July 5 alone, the grid logged more than 225 mm.
THE NUMBER ON THE MAP IS THE CONSERVATIVE ONE
These figures come from IMD's gridded rainfall, which averages rain across cells of about 25 kilometres. The grid tracks the Santacruz gauge closely on seasonal totals, but it smooths out the localised cloudbursts that flood streets. On July 26, 2005, the Santacruz gauge recorded 944 mm in 24 hours; the grid, averaging above its cell, shows 492. So, when the grid reports a record, the rain on the gauges is almost certainly heavier, not lighter.
LESS RAIN, MORE CHAOS
What has changed since 2005 is the ground the rain lands on. Greater Mumbai's built-up area has more than doubled since 1975, rising 119 per cent from 78 to 171 square kilometres, according to the European Union's Global Human Settlement Layer. Concrete does not absorb water; it speeds it into drains built for a smaller, greener city.
That is why the same rain now produces more flooding, and why a downpour smaller than 2005's can still bring the city to a halt.
The monsoon is barely a third over. Mumbai has already had its wettest start in a generation, and the wettest weeks of July and August are still ahead. The record the city should worry about is not the one already on the board. It is the one still being written.
Data notes: Rainfall figures are from IMD's 0.25° gridded daily product for the Mumbai cell.

articles.title: The India Meteorological Department (IMD) has issued an orange alert for five districts in Himachal Pradesh, warning of heavy to very heavy rainfall over the next two to three days. The weather office has also cautioned about the possibility of landslides, flash f - News Arena India
articles.search_term: ("heat advisory" OR "heat action plan" OR "heat alert" OR "IMD warning" OR "IMD alert" OR "red alert" OR "orange alert" OR "yellow alert" OR "NDMA advisory" OR "cooling shelter" OR "relief measures" OR "heat compensation" OR "severe weather warning") Andaman and Nicobar Islands
articles.full_text:
Heavy rain likely in Himachal; IMD issues orange alert
IMD Himachal Pradesh Centre Head Shobhit Katiyar on Monday said the southwest monsoon is expected to stay active across the state for at least another week. The heaviest rain is likely in the mid-hill and adjoining lower hill regions.
News Arena Network - Shimla - UPDATED: July 6, 2026, 05:47 PM - 2 min read
People cover themselves with umbrellas as they walk amid rainfall.
The India Meteorological Department (IMD) has issued an orange alert for five districts in Himachal Pradesh, warning of heavy to very heavy rainfall over the next two to three days. The weather office has also cautioned about the possibility of landslides, flash floods, rising river levels and travel disruptions.
IMD Himachal Pradesh Centre Head Shobhit Katiyar on Monday said the southwest monsoon is expected to stay active across the state for at least another week. The heaviest rain is likely in the mid-hill and adjoining lower hill regions.
"We have issued an orange alert for Kangra, Kullu, Mandi, Shimla and Solan districts for the next two to three days. Some places in these districts may receive heavy to very heavy rainfall," Katiyar said.
He said the rainy spell is expected to continue beyond July 7. Isolated heavy showers are likely between July 8 and July 10, while rainfall activity is expected to intensify again from July 11.
"Heavy rainfall activity is likely to continue till around July 12. Thereafter, between July 12 and July 16-17, the monsoon is expected to become even more active across the state. During this period, districts such as Kangra, Kullu, Mandi, Shimla, Solan and Sirmaur are likely to receive the highest rainfall," Katiyar added.
Despite the forecast, the state has so far received below-normal rainfall this monsoon season. From June 1 to July 5, Himachal recorded around 151.5 mm of rain, about 16 per cent below the normal average of 180.36 mm.
Katiyar warned that continuous rain could increase the risk of landslides and mudslides, especially in vulnerable areas. Water levels in rivers, streams and other water bodies are also expected to rise.
He advised people to stay away from rivers and streams, as heavy rain could make roads slippery, reduce visibility and increase the risk of accidents. He also said horticulture and agricultural crops could suffer partial damage in some areas.
Urging residents and tourists to remain alert, Katiyar appealed to people to follow weather advisories issued by the IMD, the state government and local district administrations.
"We request everyone to follow all weather advisories and instructions issued by local authorities so that loss of life and property can be minimised," he said.
According to the IMD, the active phase of the southwest monsoon is expected to continue across most parts of Himachal Pradesh until around July 22. The mid-hill districts are likely to receive the heaviest rainfall before the intensity gradually decreases after July 23.

मेरठ में तेज धूप और उमस भरी गर्मी का असर लोगों की सेहत पर पड़ने लगा है। गर्मी से तबीयत बिगड़ने के मामलों में बढ़ोतरी के बीच जिला अस्पताल में मरीजों की संख्या बढ़ गई है। पर्चा काउंटर पर सुबह से ही लंबी कतारें देखने को मिलीं और चिकित्सकों ने लोगों से धूप से बचाव तथा पर्याप्त पानी पीने की सलाह दी।
